title: Building with Externalities: Local Governments and Wind Farms
abstract: Does local government regulation of new infrastructure with local externalities result in efficiency? Although local governments’ choices can internalize local costs, political or contracting frictions may cause actual outcomes to deviate from the idealized benchmark of Coase (1960). I study this problem in the context of wind farms. I develop a model of interaction between households, local governments, and wind developers  where wind farms are built only if they are both profitable and allowed by local governments, who weigh local costs against payments from developers. I estimate that the average household’s cost of living three miles from a wind farm is around 7.5% of its home’s value. Each built wind farms trade off more than $7 of engineering profit for each $1 of cost to households. This arises in part because local governments must be paid roughly $3 for every $1 of externality to approve projects. Moreover, state regulations limiting payments to local governments further depress wind-farm construction. I compare the performance of alternative developer-government contracting rules in reaching the United States’ net-zero carbon goals. I find that requiring wind developers to pay local governments 20% of nearby homes’ value raises social welfare by about $220 billion relative to when developers cannot pay local governments.
appendedText: Job Market Paper, Best Student Paper at the 2025 Urban Economics Association North American Meeting

title: Churches and Local Economies
abstract: Abstract How do local churches shape economic and social life? We study this question in the Southern Baptist Convention (SBC), the nation's largest Protestant denomination, exploiting two shocks to congregational life: pastor deaths and pastor convictions for sexual abuse. Using matched-controls event study designs, we show that pastor deaths lead to church closures, while convictions generate large and persistent reductions in Baptist attendance and engagement. Both shocks reduce labor-force participation, raise unemployment, and lower nearby housing prices for several years. The shocks diverge on outcomes plausibly affected by religious beliefs rather than networks: convictions shift two-party Democratic vote share leftward and increase opioid mortality, while deaths produce no detectable changes in either. This pattern supports a framework in which deaths primarily disrupt access to congregational networks, while convictions additionally erode religious norms and beliefs that shape political and health behaviors.

title: Measuring and Mitigating Traffic Externalities
abstract: Denser housing construction can alleviate rising housing costs, but opponents frequently cite car traffic as a primary concern. We quantify these heterogeneous traffic costs from new residents across the Boston Combined Statistical Area. Using data on households’ intra- metro-area travel, the road network, road speeds, and routing decisions, we estimate monthly traffic counts on every street. We find that adding 50 typical households’ monthly car trips to the streets of housing units in the bottom 20% of baseline monthly car trips through their street decreases the value of that home by almost 7% on average, while adding that same quantity of monthly trips to the streets of housing units in the top 20% of nearby trips would decrease the values of those homes by an average of 0.04%. We estimate a structural, hedonic model of households’ residential choices and visits to points of interest (POIs) and find that households are willing to pay to avoid both car traffic on their street and travel time to POIs, and that these preferences vary widely across households. Using the model and estimates of how traffic volumes affect road speeds, we quantify the traffic externalities caused by adding new residents in different locations. We find that a Massachusetts state law targeting a 10% housing stock increase to land near public transit stops causes $3.3 billion in traffic externalities from additional residents, a 20% reduction relative to spreading those homes uniformly across space. Building those units on thoroughfares instead would decrease traffic externalities by 34% relative to the uniform allocation.

title: The Effect of Jury Selection on Convictions by Race
abstract: We use the abolition of peremptory strikes in Arizona in 2022, which eliminated attorneys' ability to strike prospective jurors without cause, to estimate how jury selection affects differences in conviction rates by race. Comparing to New Mexico, we find the difference in conviction rates between Hispanic and non-Hispanic defendants in Maricopa County drops by around 13 percentage points immediately after the removal of peremptory strikes. We find no evidence of changes in selection into trial and suggestive evidence that the effect is driven by large decreases in Hispanic defendants' relative likelihood of conviction among judges for whom it was previously the largest.
